speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 3rd, 1996. Janet Rogowsky-- maiden name, Eisenberg-- is being interviewed by Betsy Stern in Mamaroneck, New York, United States, in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Today is May 3rd, 1996. Janet Rogowsky-- maiden name, Eisenberg-- is being interviewed by me, Betsy Stern-- S-T-E-R-N-- in Mamaroneck, New York, United States, in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Janet, let's start by your telling us your parents' names and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Shamai, and my mother's was Miriam. Her maiden name, Segal. I was born in Ostrog, Poland, a small town on the border of Russia and Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was January 15, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are some of your earliest memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your earliest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, earliest. Well, my home and my upbringing, and the charming little town that I spent my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start with your family. You had brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had two brothers and two sisters. I was the youngest in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the town-- what was Ostrog like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrog was really a cultural center of the entire area. It had a lot of Jewish scholars, and they had a big yeshiva, and they had a religious school. And a lot of prominent rabbis and scholars would come and study in Ostrog. It goes back centuries. Since the eighth-- eighth century, there are documents that Jews came to Ostrog. As a matter of fact, they were persecuted by the Germans and tried to escape, and came to the town. It was a very cultural town. We had a Yiddish theatre, a regular theatre. It had a movie house. It was-- it had a very good high school and two elementary schools. And even the public school, the public high school, was paid. You had to pay tuition. So only those kids who could afford would go to that school. So the standard was really very high. And I felt that I had a very good education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how long your family had lived in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that my parents came-- I don't even know if they came from somewhere else, but I know that they were there, already, before 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talked about the act of Jewish life in Ostrog, was your family a part of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was part in the sense that my father was the honorary president of the synagogue, and he was an honorary chairman of the city council, and he was always involved in helping poor people. Not only Jews, but-- and I remember that, anytime we had a Jewish holiday, from-- from our estate, from-- from the farm, a truck carried [INAUDIBLE] poultry and dairy would come in and distribute to-- to Jews who were in need. And during the year, he would help a lot of people, regardless of religion. So he was really very known, and he was always anxious to help people. And of course, he had a terrific influence on-- on us, certainly on me, because he always tried to show us that money's not very important. There's nothing to be proud of if you have a little bit more money. And I will never forget. Any time I was in school and we wanted to go away, there was, like, an excursion. He would always say, make sure to take along one kid who cannot afford to go with you. When I want to go to the movies, instead of $1, let's say, he would say, here's $2. Make sure to take someone along with you. And I will always remember that he would take a little coin and put it on the edge of the table and push it. And he said, you see, I had money. And I just pushed it and the money disappeared. It fell off. So you have money today, but tomorrow you might not have it. So don't take-- don't get attached to the value of money. And that I always remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you describe, obviously, your family as being a very wealthy one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a home in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said an estate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Wilia, which was-- it's a village 25 kilometers from Ostrog. So we would spend our vacation in-- in the winter and the entire summer. And we were five children. So each of us had the permission to bring one friend. And then we had, always, relatives beside that, with us in the summer. So we had fun. And we were, already, 10. And at the same time, the-- the minister-- the Orthodox minister had two children. So-- And there was a Jewish family. He was an owner of a mill. He had a child, so we-- so those were the children that we played with. And it was fun. But we were always anxious during the summer to go back to Ostrog, because there were kind of dances, and kids were having fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually, we were not happy that we are so long away from all our friends. So sometimes, we were cheating a little bit, and-- and my father never wanted us to go back during vacation. So since I was the youngest and my friend and my sister would say, now play sick. We'll have to go to the doctor. So of course, I tried not to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they really convinced me. So finally, I would, for a whole evening I would moan, I have a toothache. And my father didn't believe it, so I had to get the support from the girl who was taking care of me. And she came to my father and says, she's really in pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we finally-- we'd get permission to go for two days. So they would have to drive us. And of course, it was by carriage with horses. And-- but in general, it was really a lot of fun. Now, how did your father have the estate managed? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was there all the time. He came home only weekends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he would come to Ostrog on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrog only for the weekends. And my oldest father-- my oldest brother, after law school in Warsaw, came to-- to the estate. He became my father's assistant. And then we had a regular man who was in charge of the entire estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the estate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands of acres. It was a big one. I really don't know exactly how much, because a lot of woods were involved. He raised the horses for racing. There was a lot of cattle. And there was a lot of-- a lot of fields. So as a matter of fact, when you were approaching the Wilia, you saw it from a long, long distance, from far away. It was quite large, practically the whole village. And it was very nice that, any time we came for vacation, and if school was still in session in Wilia, the [INAUDIBLE] the teacher knew when we were coming, so she gave permission to the little kids to go out and greet us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as we were approaching the school, all of a sudden, we saw little kids running out and waving their hand and-- and we came down in the carriage and talked with them and went to town. So that was really very nice. Was that unusual, for Jews to own an estate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of this kind of estate, yes. It was unusual. And Ostrog itself was not a very rich town. A lot of poor Jews living there. A lot of poor people living there. And everybody think that all Jews were rich, and some of them were really struggling. They were cobblers. They were butchers. There were a lot of peddlers. And they had a hard time making a living, very, very small stores. So without the financial support from others, they had a hard time. But they were very proud that they make a living. But in general, it was not a rich town. It was a cultural town. And there was a group-- there was a class of people, [INAUDIBLE] was very comfortable. But of course, the younger people would leave Ostrog and go to study in other places. Some of the universities in Poland you couldn't get in because you were a Jew. So they would go either to Czechoslovakia or to Lithuania. And some of them would go to France. So-- but they came back. And if they were doctors, they would establish a practice in Ostrog. And-- so the intelligentsia really added to the flavor of this town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say intelligentsia, are you talking just Jewish intelligentsia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about the Jewish intelligentsia, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it being a rich Jew in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you, I really never, never had felt any antisemitism in Poland, personally. Because as I said, my father was very, very good to everybody in town, and we were never snobs. We never-- we always had all kind of children in our house. I used to go with my friends to their church, because they invited me. They would come to the synagogue if I went. When we had a Christmas tree, they always invited me. I went there. And that way helped me a lot to survive as a Christian, because I was with them. So I-- I never-- as a matter of fact-- and there's a custom that, New Year's Eve, you will come to homes and you would wish people a happy New Year. And the fire department or the police would always go to people that they wanted to wish a happy New Year. And we were always the first. At midnight, we were the first house that they would come and-- and wish us a happy New Year. And father was very nice to them too. So I really didn't-- of course I knew that there was anti-Semitism in Warsaw. But my--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my brother was studying in Warsaw. And of course, he never wanted to admit it to us and-- but I learned later that mother knew, though she didn't didn't-- didn't tell us anything because she didn't want the children to worry about it. But-- and of course, in Warsaw at that time, there were all kind of restrictions. First of all, it was unusual for a Jewish guy-- man-- to get into-- to law school, and it was very expensive. And they could not sit on the same side with the students. The Jews had to sit separately, to the left. And very often there they encountered, already, antisemitism They couldn't live in the same dorm with Polish. They had their Jewish quarters. And very often, some Jewish students were beat up without any reason. So in the bigger cities, and especially among students, you already knew that there was antisemitism But personally, the family never cared. Never happened to us. It is true that among peasants, especially Ukrainian peasants, there was a lot of antisemitism But the fear of a land loaner was so high that they wouldn't dare to show it-- show it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was the land owner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was the landlord. But he was the so-called very good landlord, but still a landlord. As a matter of fact, when the war broke out and my father was afraid of the Russians-- because he was afraid he'd be sent to Siberia as a land owner-- some peasants came from Wilia to Ostrog, walking 25 kilometers, begging my father not to go away, and they will hide him. And they will build-- they will dig a ditch, and they will hide entire family. And he said, look, it's enough to have one enemy there. And-- and they-- they might-- all might wish me well. But one of them might betray me, and I don't trust them. So my father knew that, among them, there are some people who would easily tell about-- about him. And of course, he was proven to be right. Because we see now, that even if you saw the film, I don't know if you saw it on television a few weeks-- they show how when the Jew comes back to Poland, they're very happy to see him. But there are some Jews-- some Poles who will even now say that others told about the Jews and that's how the Germans got them. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family get to such a position of prominence?

speaker: interviewee
text: How my family got--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your-- how did your family become landowners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from what I just read to you, is my grandfather was working for nobility, for the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, for the [NON-ENGLISH]. I mean, the people who came from royalty. And Jews were not allowed to buy any land at that time. But they-- they were very fond of my grandfather. And then my grandfather was very anxious to train his sons to-- to understand what a farm is. So my father, at the age of 14, was already working with my grandfather. And-- and then just, I guess, he was allowed to buy a small piece of land. And that's how he enlarged it. And he was very industrious and very creative. And it wasn't forbidden anymore, for people to own-- for Jews to own land.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had said that your father was very active in the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, active in the sense that he was helping financially. But he was never there. So he really couldn't act-- be very active.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your Jewish life, when you lived in Ostrog during the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all took Hebrew. We had a private tutor who taught us Hebrew. My brothers had bar mitzvah. They had Jewish weddings. And my family always went for the High Holidays, to the synagogue-- well, holidays to the synagogue. And they made sure that the children go to the synagogue. My mother was kosher, and we had two sinks in the kitchen. And-- and she always stuck to it. My father was not. My father was more Reformed, because he traveled a lot, and-- and he came from a different kind of a home. And they never really forced us to do anything. So if we wanted to eat non-kosher food, as long as we are not doing it in the house, my mother made believe that she didn't see it. And-- and then we had a lot of non-Jewish friends, and a lot of Jewish friends. So it was really a traditional home, but not a religious home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother light candles on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She lit candles every Friday night. As a matter of fact, every Friday night we would eat in our formal dining room because we had two dining rooms. And she really made sure that everybody's at the table Friday night, and-- and that was really very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father make Kiddush?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But we fasted on Yom Kippur. And my younger brother once almost fainted, he was-- and yet he refused to eat. Even my mother said, you can eat already. But he says, no. I will take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you went to school in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your school years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very strict. I-- I liked school because it was also fun, [? then that ?] we saw all our friends. And we had gym every day, and I enjoyed that. So-- so that was very good. And-- and-- but it was strict, and a lot of discipline. And teachers, still, if you made a mistake, would hit you with a ruler.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they instilled a little bit of fear in the kid-- kids. And when I look back at it, I don't care for it. And maybe that's why I treated my own students differently than how I was treated. But-- and probably, some of them didn't care for-- for Jews, among the faculty.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish teacher could not get a position in high school. I know only of one case, only one Jew got a teaching position in elementary school. But-- oh, in the-- there was a seminar for-- for teachers, a two-year school. So there, occasionally, would have a Jewish teacher. But otherwise, nothing. Not in gymnasium. Not in the high school. So-- and we took a lot of courses. But it was different. We did not take chemistry every day. We take chemistry three days and physics two days a week. So we could squeeze in more subjects. So we took about 10, 11 subjects a year. And-- but it was a very traditional learning. But I guess that all over the world at that time, people didn't have this kind of approach to education as now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about after school? Did you belong to organizations? Sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to the Betar. But this was only over the weekend, because during the week, we really had to work on our homework. There was a lot of homework, and there was no playing around during the week. All the activities were taking place during the weekend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about Betar.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- you had all kinds of speakers who came and talked to us. And as I told you, Jabotinsky was in our house, and so was Begin. And they-- and they enjoyed coming to Ostrog because there was a lot of Zionists there. And most, even the older people, belonged to Zionist organization because they were really persecuted in Russia. And they remembered that during the Czar, this was clandestine. They could not say that they belonged because they would have been shot. The same thing during the Stalin years.

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to make clear that it was in the years between the two World Wars that Ostrog became part of Poland, not part of Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1920.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And my father was even part of the commission that established the border. As a matter of fact, he was the one who met, at the station, General Pilsudski, who was a big hero-- one of the greatest heroes of Poland-- who was invited to establish the border between Russia and Poland. And since our estate was on the border, he was very much interested to be involved in it, my father, because half of the estate really went to Russia. And if he didn't go with them, he probably would have lost most of his estate. So he was the one who met this general at the railroad station, with his horses and carriage. And that's how he saved some of his estate. But it was in 1920, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Jabotinsky and Begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Jabotinsky was a little bit aloof. But he was one of the greatest orators, one of the greatest speakers I've ever heard. And Begin was very friendly and very, very bright. Very sharp. Extremely popular all over Poland. And everybody predicted a great future for him. And I met, also, his wife. Because later on when I  went to Lvov, I looked him up. And we met. And then I met, also, his wife and-- but I never met his children. He had two children. So he really was a very, very prominent man. And of course, we had a formal staircase in our home, so when we had dignitaries like Jabotinsky or-- or Begin, or other Polish dignitaries, we would have a guard in front of the-- of the entrance. Because with Jabotinsky and Begin, they were always in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- there were formal eve-- formal evenings, then, when they would come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh yes. At that time, usually we had a big dinner and some official would come. My brother was-- I think he was the president of the Revisionist Party, of the Betar, with the younger ones. But if he wasn't the president, he certainly was involved. I think at one time he was the president of the organization. And then we had a few presidents of the organization that-- who were searched by the Russians, because the Communists was trying to destroy the Zionists. So-- so as a matter of fact, my brother hid one that they were looking for because they felt that he was a danger. He was very known, already, in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were aware that all this was going on at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time, no. You know, it's different. Nowadays, little children, parents share information. That was not the case in my home. It-- as a matter of fact, any time my parents went out, I never heard my mother saying, come with us. Only when you went to relatives occasionally, or to grandparents. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had our own friends, and they had their own friends. The only thing is they always encourage us to invite friends to our house. And anytime we wanted to go to someone's house, mother said, but you have so much room. Why don't you invite them? And she said, I like to know where you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you can invite anybody you want. But stay home. So they really tried to protect us a little bit. And I will never forget, and I still have a picture, when I wanted to take up skiing. So they finally bought me skis, but I could only ski in the backyard of my house because she was afraid I would hurt myself. I mean, so they were a little bit-- maybe because I was the youngest. And I was always working very hard in school. I always tried to do my best. So I was left alone.

words.text: so they were a little bit--

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the years that you remember were good years. Do you remember when things started to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things started to change in '39. That became a disaster. Because THE-- the first day of '39, in September, my father left because he was really scared of-- of the Russians. And he went to Lvov, and changed his name. And he rented one room from a Pole who happened to be a big antisemite. He was a cripple. He was in a wheelchair. And-- but he rented out rooms because they just couldn't live on what they had. It was very difficult for the Poles at that time, economically. And then he sent for the family. So they decided that my-- my mother, my-- my unmarried sister, and my unmarried brother will come and stay with him in the room and see what happens. And I was going to remain with my married sister, whose husband was an accountant. And they realized that nothing will happen to him since he's an accountant, he's working for the city, that they will not say that he is the capitalist.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with my sister and her family, husband, and two children. And-- until they realized that things are getting very bad. And then in 1940, they sent a messenger to Ostrog and I came to Lvov, to join them. When you say things were getting bad, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- well, they started to talk about the-- about the Germans having labor camps. We never knew anything. Until Poland was occupied, nobody knew about concentration camps. So-- so la-- so nobody wanted to, of course, be in a labor camp either. But they felt, well, that is not the worst thing. Because you-- you would go for the day, and you come back in the evening. That was the idea. But other-- otherwise, we didn't know anything-- anything else about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is 1940, that you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that I joined my par-- my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they still living in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still live in one room and we still had friends. Because my older brother was taken in-- in '41, my older brother was taken to the Red Army. So he left a wife and child.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he did not want her to be alone, so she came to live with us. And then she had a friend that ran away, and she didn't have any place, so she also-- so we all slept on the floor. But only my parents had one, like, a twin bed. And everybody else was sleeping on the floor. Do you know why you were living in one room? Was-- did your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have  any money.

speaker: interviewer
text: --couldn't have access to any money?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any money. No. First of all, we had no money because, for a land owner, no matter what you had, you put in toward-- to buy more cattle, to buy more a product. And-- and of course, he had never saved. And as a matter of fact, about a week or two weeks before the war broke out, he bought land in woods in another part of the province, and spent a lot of money, gave a big down payment, and-- because he felt this is the right time to buy. Because he didn't believe there's going to be this kind of a disaster, and after the war, it's going to be much more. It was the very poor investment. So he spent a lot of money on that. And we did-- and mother had an awful lot of jewelry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we lived on that. We spent-- she used to sell a diamond at a time and we lived on that.
